title: The 'Feathered Helmets' of the Sea Peoples: Joining the Iconographic and Archaeological Evidence (Talanta 44, pp. 27–40)
author: Assaf Yasur-Landau
publisher: Talanta: Proceedings of the Dutch Archaeological and Historical Society, vol. 44
allowedUseNotes: Copyrighted journal article. Summaries in project's own words only; no image/plate reproductions.
summary: Peer-reviewed synthesis joining the Egyptian iconographic record (Medinet Habu reliefs of Ramesses III, c. 1175 BCE) with independent archaeological attestations of the same feathered/plumed headdress in the Aegean, Cyprus, and the Levant. Establishes that the motif does not appear in Egyptian art before or after the 12th century BCE, but also traces its reproduction outside Egyptian propaganda art — including in locally made Philistine material culture on the southern coastal plain (e.g., a bronze chariot linchpin from Tel Miqne-Ekron) — meaning the headdress is corroborated as a genuine, self-reproduced Philistine/Sea-Peoples marker rather than solely an Egyptian artistic invention.
confidenceNotes: Bibliographic details (journal, volume, page range 27–40) and content summary confirmed via multiple corroborating search-engine snippets/abstracts describing the article's argument; the article's own PDF and academia.edu mirror both returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch, so specific internal page numbers for individual claims (e.g., exactly where the Ekron linchpin is discussed) are TO VERIFY against a primary copy. The article-level bibliographic identification and its headline finding (attested range does not extend before/after the 12th century BCE in Egyptian art, but is independently corroborated in Levantine/Aegean artifacts) are corroborated across independent search results and treated as reliable at that level.
